title: Why Vocabulary Lists Are Failing Your Students (And What to Do Instead)
excerpt: After years of handing out word lists, I started asking: are my students actually retaining this vocabulary? The answer forced me to rethink everything about how I teach words.
content:
It is a Monday morning ritual familiar to every language teacher: hand out the vocabulary list, tell students to study it for the test on Friday, and move on. For years, I did the same. And for years, I saw the same result — students who could pass the test and forget every word by Saturday.

The problem is not the students. The problem is the decontextualised word list itself.

What cognitive science tells us

Memory researchers have known for decades that we do not store words in isolation — we store them embedded in networks of meaning, association, and context. When you learn the word ubiquitous next to its dictionary definition, you have one memory trace. When you encounter it in a vivid sentence about smartphones in classrooms, you have a trace connected to imagery, grammar, emotion, and situation.

"The strength of a memory is directly related to the depth of processing at the time of encoding." — Craik & Lockhart (1972)

This is Craik and Lockhart's famous levels of processing framework, and it explains why rote vocabulary lists produce shallow retention.

Three classroom shifts that work

1. Teach words in rich sentences, not isolated

Instead of: ubiquitous — adjective — found everywhere
Try: "Smartphones have become so ubiquitous in modern classrooms that teachers must now design lessons that leverage rather than ban them."

The sentence provides grammar, collocation, register, and a concrete situation — all for free.

2. Create retrieval opportunities, not repetition

The spacing effect and retrieval practice are among the most robust findings in learning science. Rather than reviewing words three days in a row, space the review across days 1, 3, 7, and 21. And instead of re-reading the definition, force recall: cover the word and retrieve it from the sentence.

3. Build a living vocabulary record

Encourage students to keep a vocabulary notebook that records: the word, the original sentence they found it in, their own new sentence, a visual or association, and any useful collocations. This is metacognition made visible.

What this means for my teaching

I have redesigned my vocabulary instruction around the principle of contextualised, spaced, and retrieved learning. The vocabulary section of this website is a direct reflection of this — every word is presented inside a real sentence from the domain it belongs to, with usage notes that go beyond the dictionary.

It is slow, but it works. And I would rather my students leave the course able to use ten words precisely than forget a hundred.

title: AI in the ELT Classroom: Tool or Threat?
excerpt: ChatGPT arrived in our classrooms uninvited. Two years on, I've stopped trying to ban it and started asking a better question: what does AI change about what language learning actually means?
content:
When ChatGPT launched in late 2022, the first reaction in many TESOL circles was panic: students will just use AI to write their essays. The second reaction, a little later, was a different kind of panic: if AI can write better English than most of our students, what exactly are we teaching them?

Both reactions are understandable, but I think both miss the more interesting question.

The honest question we avoid

Here is what I think we need to ask: What is the purpose of language learning if the linguistic barrier can be lowered by a tool?

This is not a new question. Calculators did not eliminate the need for mathematical reasoning; they changed what it means to be mathematically competent. Word processors did not eliminate writing skills; they changed which writing skills matter.

AI is doing the same thing to language — raising the floor, but also, potentially, raising the ceiling.

What AI is genuinely good at in language learning

  • Unlimited low-stakes practice: AI conversation partners give anxious learners a private space to practise without fear of judgement.
  • Instant, patient feedback: A learner can ask "Is this sentence correct?" at 2am and get a thoughtful answer.
  • Personalised input: AI can generate texts at exactly the right level, on exactly the topic a learner is motivated by.

What AI cannot do

AI cannot replicate the social and emotional dimensions of language use. It cannot give a learner the experience of successfully negotiating meaning with a real human being, of being understood across a cultural gap, of building a relationship through a second language. Those experiences are irreplaceable — and they are, arguably, the reason people learn languages.

My position: AI is a powerful practice tool and an equity equaliser, but it does not replace the teacher. It changes what the teacher needs to focus on — less on drilling correct forms, more on building communicative competence and critical language awareness.

What I'm doing in my own classroom

I am experimenting with using AI to generate first drafts that students then critically evaluate and revise. This flips the AI from a cheating tool into a thinking tool — students must recognise what the AI got wrong, what it missed, and why their own voice matters.

It is early days. But I would rather be shaping how AI enters my classroom than pretending it has not arrived.

title: Motivation in the Language Classroom: Beyond Gold Stars
excerpt: The most motivated language learners I've taught weren't motivated by grades. Understanding why changes everything about how we design learning experiences.
content:
Every language teacher has met them: the student who studies for hours outside class not because they have to, but because they want to. And we have all met the opposite — the student who is technically present but learning nothing, going through the motions.

What is the difference? The tempting answer is ability. The more interesting answer is motivation — and specifically, what kind of motivation is driving the learning.

Two very different kinds of 'wanting to learn'

Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985) gives us a useful framework. At one end of the motivation spectrum is extrinsic motivation: studying because of external pressure — grades, parents, job requirements. At the other end is intrinsic motivation: studying because the activity itself is interesting, satisfying, or meaningful.

The research finding that most changes how I think about teaching is this: extrinsic rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation over time. When you pay children to read books they previously enjoyed for free, they stop enjoying reading. The external reward crowds out the internal one.

The ideal L2 self

Zoltán Dörnyei's influential L2 Motivational Self System adds another dimension. Learners are motivated, he argues, by the gap between their current self and their ideal L2 self — the vivid image of themselves as a proficient speaker of the target language.

This has practical implications. One of the most powerful things a teacher can do is help students construct that ideal self image: through exposure to inspiring models, through success experiences, through helping students connect language learning to their deeper life goals.

What this means for IELTS preparation

IELTS preparation is a classic case of extrinsic motivation — students are studying for a number, not for love of English. My goal in such courses is to create pockets of intrinsic motivation within an extrinsically motivated context: texts that genuinely interest students, tasks that feel meaningful, moments where language connects to something real in their lives.

It is not always possible. But when it happens, you see a different kind of learning in the room.
